perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of loss or dama ge to the Providing Party as a result of breach of the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall be liable only in respect of loss or damage resulting directly from the breach, and no liability will be accepted in respect of any special, indi rect, incidental or consequential losses or damages whatsoever (including, but not limited to, lost profits or revenue) suffered by the Providing Party or any person connected with the Providing Party, arising out of the performance or breach of this Agree ment, whether claims for said losses or damages are premised on contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of loss or damage to the Providing Party as a result of breach of the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall be liable for all loss or damage resulting from the breach, even if such damages are indirect, incidental, or consequential losses or damages whatsoever (including, but not limited to, lost profits or revenue) suffered by the Providing Party or any person connected with the Providing Party, arising out of the performance or breach of this Agreement, whether claims for said losses or damages are premised on contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the exclusion of liability for indirect, incidental, or consequential damages. This creates a potentially unlimited liability, conflicting with standard contractual limitations and established legal principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA) (UK Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: UCTA section 3 limits the extent to which one party can exclude or restrict liability for breach of contract or negligence. While not an outright prohibition, it subjects such exclusions to a 'reasonableness' test. By removing the exclusion for consequential losses entirely, and stating that the receiving party will be liable 'for all loss or damage', the clause might be deemed unreasonable under UCTA, especially when considering the relative bargaining positions of the parties, and other factors. This alteration aims to nullify the original protective intent and renders the contract potentially non-compliant. The new clause makes the Receiving Party completely liable, potentially conflicting with the UCTA provisions regarding the reasonableness of contract terms.

perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the original contract's limitation of liability for indirect, incidental, or consequential damages, making the Receiving Party liable for 'all loss or damage'. The LLM explanation suggests this alteration might conflict with UCTA provisions regarding the reasonableness of contract terms, particularly concerning the exclusion of consequential losses. While the provided scraped snippets are unhelpful (one is missing, and the other is a 'Not found' error), the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation both indicate a potential contradiction based on reasonableness and standard contractual limitations by expanding liability beyond direct damages.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Information will be used solely for giving effect to the Business Under Discussion and further that it will not disclose to any person (i) the Information and (ii) the fact that the Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or that the Receiving Party have inspected any portion of the Information, for an y reason or purpose whatsoever, unless the Providing Party has consented in writing to such disclosure or except as stated otherwise herein or as required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request. The term “person” as used in these provisions shall be broadly interpreted to include without limitation any corporation, company, partnership, individual or group.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Information will be used solely for giving effect to the Business Under Discussion and further that it will not disclose to any person (i) the Information and (ii) the fact that the Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or that the Receiving Party have inspected any portion of the Information, for any reason or purpose whatsoever, unless the Providing Party has consented in writing to such disclosure. Disclosure required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request is strictly prohibited under all circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This revision directly prohibits disclosure even when legally mandated. This is a critical contradiction because it forces the Receiving Party to violate legal and regulatory requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6(1)(c) of the GDPR states that processing (which includes disclosure) of personal data is lawful if 'processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject'. The modified clause explicitly prohibits disclosures required by law, directly contravening this provision of the GDPR, making the contract unenforceable to the extent it prevents compliance with GDPR obligations. Partner Ireland Insurance Limited would likely be subject to GDPR as they are based in Dublin, Ireland.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 6 GDPR – Lawfulness of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that at least one of the following applies: the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes; processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract; processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject; processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child. Point (f) of the first subparagraph shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. Member States may maintain or introduce more specific provisions to adapt the application of the rules of this Regulation with regard to processing for compliance with points (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 by determining more precisely specific requirements for the processing and other measures to ensure lawful and fair processing including for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 1 The basis for the processing referred to in point (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 shall be laid down by: Union law; or Member State law to which the controller is subject. 2 The purpose of the processing shall be determined in that legal basis or, as regards the processing referred to in point (e) of paragraph 1, shall be necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 3 That legal basis may contain specific provisions to adapt the application of rules of this Regulation, inter alia: the general conditions governing the lawfulness of processing by the controller; the types of data which are subject to the processing; the data subjects concerned; the entities to, and the purposes for which, the personal data may be disclosed; the purpose limitation; storage periods; and processing operations and processing procedures, including measures to ensure lawful and fair processing such as those for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 4 The Union or the Member State law shall meet an objective of public interest and be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued. Where the processing for a purpose other than that for which the personal data have been collected is not based on the data subject’s consent or on a Union or Member State law which constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard the objectives referred to in Article 23 (1), the controller shall, in order to ascertain whether processing for another purpose is compatible with the purpose for which the personal data are initially collected, take into account, inter alia: any link between the purposes for which the personal data have been collected and
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the GDPR as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text allowed disclosure as required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request. The changed text explicitly prohibits disclosure even when mandated by law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 'Art. 6 GDPR – Lawfulness of processing', states that processing (which includes disclosure) is lawful if 'processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject'. The modified clause contravenes this provision by prohibiting disclosures required by law, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In th e event that the Receiving Party is required by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Receiving Party agrees that it will provide the Providing Party with prompt written notice of such request(s) prior to the required disclosure and the Providing Party shall use reasonable efforts, at its own expense, to seek a protective o rder or other appropriate remedy. If a protective order or other remedy is not obtained and a waiver hereunder is not received, the Receiving Party shall disclose only that p ortion of the Information which is legally required and shall use its best effort s to obtain assurances that confidential treatment will be accorded to such Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party believes it is required by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Receiving Party is not required to notify the Providing Party and may disclose the full extent of the Information required. The Receiving Party bears no responsibility to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy. If a protective order or other remedy is not obtained and a waiver hereunder is not received, the Receiving Party shall disclose all of the Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the obligation to notify the Providing Party of a disclosure request and also states that they may disclose the full information. The original language attempts to limit the disclosure to what is legally required, but the new modification removes this safeguard.
perturbation.law_explanation: CPR Rule 31.22 implies restrictions on the use of disclosed documents, meaning they can only be used for the specific proceedings unless the court permits otherwise or the document has been read in court. The amended clause, by allowing the Receiving Party to disclose all information without notifying the Providing Party, potentially contradicts the implied restrictions on further use or disclosure enshrined within the CPR. The amendment facilitates uncontrolled information release, possibly running against the principles governing disclosure in legal proceedings under CPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: discovery | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you discovery In civil actions , the discovery process refers to what parties use during pre-trial to gather information in preparation for trial . The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure have very liberal discovery provisions. Before the rules were adopted in 1938, plaintiffs essentially had to prove their case before filing suit . See notice pleading . The Federal Rules changed that such that under the rules' liberal discovery approach, plaintiffs who strongly suspect that they were wronged can file a lawsuit, even if they do not have solid evidence . Additionally, during discovery, they can force the defendant to give them evidence that they can use to build their case. Discovery under the Federal Rules is very broad. According to Rule 26(b)(1) , "Parties may obtain discovery regarding any non-privileged matter that is relevant to any party's claim or defense." The federal rules also provide several tools that can be used to get information from other parties, including interrogatories , depositions , and requests for admission . A party may also compel other parties to give them access to documents, real property , or other things for review or testing. See Rules 26-37 . Complying with discovery rules is particularly difficult and expensive for institutional defendants because it takes time and incurs legal fees . This difficulty is somewhat mitigated by rules allowing defendants to simply grant plaintiffs access to their records, effectively telling them "if you want it, find it for yourself." See Rule 33 . This does not, however, reduce the legal expenses involved in reviewing and responding to discovery requests. Depositions are particularly expensive. In most states, an attorney’s work product is not obtainable through disclosure. This is an unqualified immunity regardless of subject matter. “ Material prepared for litigation ” is not absolutely protected, however. That generally means that work prepared by non-attorneys for the litigation would be discoverable. For example, in People v. Kozlowski, 11 N.Y.3d 223 (2008) , notes from director interviews taken during a law firm’s internal investigation were not protected from a subpoena filed by the defendants. Discovery rules vary by jurisdiction. See: State Civil Procedure Rules . [Last reviewed in September of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS class actions courts criminal law criminal procedure evidence judicial administration legal practice/ethics wex articles civil procedure courts and procedure Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Receiving Party's obligation to notify the Providing Party before disclosing information and allows for full disclosure, which directly contradicts the original agreement's intent to limit disclosure and seek protective measures. The Law Explanation suggests this may contradict restrictions on further use or disclosure. Although the Scraped Law Snippet does not explicitly mention a 'duty to notify', the altered contract removes all obligations to protect the providing party's information. By removing the 'duty to notify' it opens the door to full disclosure which could be damaging. This represents a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' because the new terms do not align with the original intent of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed in acc ordance with the laws of England and Wales without giving effect to conflict of law principles, and the Parties agree to submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the English courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed in accordance with the laws of Ireland without giving effect to conflict of law principles, and the Parties agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Irish courts.
perturbation.explanation: Switching the governing law from England and Wales to Ireland and changing the jurisdiction to be exclusive in Ireland may cause potential conflicts.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Brussels Recast Regulation concerns jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters. Article 25 deals with choice of court agreements. While parties can agree on a jurisdiction, the regulation sets out rules for when such agreements are valid, and the chosen court usually has exclusive jurisdiction. However, if the contract involves parties from different EU member states (e.g., an Irish company and an English individual), switching to exclusive Irish jurisdiction might conflict with the reasonable expectations based on prior dealings, established business locations, or the initial agreement referencing English law. This change in jurisdiction can affect the enforceability of the contract in other EU countries, due to the regulations on recognition and enforcement of judgments.
perturbation.justification: The change from English to Irish law, coupled with the shift to exclusive Irish jurisdiction, creates a potential conflict with the Brussels Recast Regulation as explained by the LLM. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly related, the Law Explanation suggests that switching jurisdiction, especially to exclusive jurisdiction, can impact the enforceability of the contract in other EU countries, particularly if there are parties from different member states involved. This misalignment of terminology, specifically regarding jurisdiction, can render the contract unenforceable in certain situations. The original contract specified England and Wales. Changing it to Ireland, introduces a misalignment with reasonable expectations, creating a legal contradiction.
